sanskrit_devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते ।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद्दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति ॥ ४० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca |
pārtha naiveha nāmutra vināśas tasya vidyate |
na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścid durgatiṁ tāta gacchati || 40 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Son of Pṛthā, a transcendentalist engaged in auspicious activities does not meet with destruction either in this world or in the spiritual world; one who does good, My friend, is never overcome by evil.

The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Son of Pritha, a transcendentalist engaged in auspicious activities does not meet with destruction either in this world or in the spiritual world; one who does good, My friend, is never overcome by evil.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.40 O Partha, eva vidyate, there is certainly; na vinasah, no ruin; tasya, for him; iha, here, in this world; or amutra, hereafter, in the other world. commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.40 The Lord said — Neither here nor there is destruction for him who has begun Yoga with faith and has then fallen away from it. The meaning is that there is no destruction either in the form of failure of attainment of desires or in the form of Pratyavaya, which means the attainment of
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-40-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

positions.rendering: one who does good is never overcome by evil
he who is established in virtuous action does not go to a bad state — the yoga-aspirant being engaged in the highest good
the doer-of-good — the one who attempted yoga out of faith — is not destroyed; the yoga-attempt itself is kalyāṇa

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings agree: the yoga-attempt itself counts as a 'good deed' — kalyāṇa-kṛt is not a separate category from the fallen yogi but a description of what the fallen yogi has been doing. Therefore the consolation is structural, not exceptional: the work counts even when the work fails to reach its perfection.

literal_meaning: The Blessed Lord said: O Pārtha, neither in this world nor in the next is there destruction for him. For no one who does good, dear one, comes to a bad end.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.40 is the consolation — one of the most-quoted verses in the Gita and the most-loved in the chapter. The structure is sharp: naiva iha na amutra vināśas tasya vidyate. The doubled negation forecloses both worries (this-life perish + next-life fall). Then the universal: na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścid durgatim gacchati — no doer-of-good ever goes to a bad destination. The vocative tāta (dear one, often translated 'my son' or 'dear child') is one of the most affectionate addresses in the entire Gita. Sankara's reading: he who is established in virtuous action does not go to a bad state; the yoga-aspirant has been engaged in the highest good — therefore a fortiori — and his attempt cannot lead to perdition. Ramanuja's reading: the doer-of-good — the one who attempted yoga out of faith — is not destroyed; the yoga-attempt itself is kalyāṇa, an act of supreme good. Both traditions preserve the structural force: the genuine attempt at yoga is itself an act of good and cannot be erased. This verse is the chapter's emotional center.
